digest.top_line: EXTEND: MDT+SOC cuts PFS risk across baskets, HR 0.54 (0.41-0.72) p<0.001, with RT delivering 98% of the metastasis-directed treatment.
digest.tldr: RT's reach widened (EXTEND's multi-histology MDT signal, FASTRACK II's 100% 84-mo local control, 4,913-pt HCC EBRT cohort at resection-comparable OS) while volume and fractionation questions sharpened: PEACE 2 pelvic nodal RT missed cPFS, HEAT put 5fx on par with 26fx, NRG 1005 folded the boost into 15 fractions. Oligomet, prostate, and head-neck carried the day.

sites.intro: OPERA's post-hoc W14 read suggests near-complete response is good enough to select for watch-and-wait, moving the assessment 10 weeks earlier.

studies.name: OPERA Trial (5-year)
studies.tldr: 76% achieved a good clinical response at W14 (DRE+rectoscopy); nCR matched cCR for 5yr organ preservation (77% vs 81%), enabling early W&W selection.

details.text: 📊 W14 clinical response by arm and 5yr organ preservation

details: 📊 W14 CTRE feasible in 122/141 (87%): 76% good response (cCR+nCR), 24% PR
📊 5yr organ preservation similar for cCR vs nCR: 81% vs 77%
📐 Clinical CR concordant with MRI: TRG1-2 in 98% (80/82)
🔍 Post-hoc analysis testing W14 (~1mo post-NAT) vs protocol W24 assessment for W&W selection
⚠️ nCR reflects RT-related mucosal change, not residual tumor (per authors)
⚠️ Per authors, nCR should not justify radical surgery
⚠️ Post-hoc, non-randomised response comparison; W14 selection not prospectively validated vs W24

verdict.rationale: Post-hoc analysis of an alternate (W14 vs protocol W24) timepoint; response-group comparison non-randomised and not prospectively validated for W&W selection.

studies.significance: nCR carries the same 5yr organ-preservation prognosis as cCR (77% vs 81%) and reflects RT-related mucosal change, not residual tumor, so an nCR at reassessment shouldn't route a patient to salvage TME. Early W14 clinical exam (DRE+rectoscopy) matched MRI TRG1-2 in 98%, letting W&W selection move up from W24.
studies.monday_clinic: In a rectal-cancer pt with a near-complete clinical response after chemoradiotherapy, this supports continued watch-and-wait over radical TME; the 24% with only a partial response stay surgical candidates and aren't covered.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Post-hoc analysis of the randomised **OPERA** trial, **N=141** evaluable. Tests an **earlier** response timepoint (**W14**, ~1 mo after NAT end) against the protocol **W24** triad, scoring clinical tumor response (CTRE) by **DRE + rectoscopy** as cCR / nCR / PR.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: OPERA randomised the boost after neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy (EBRT boost vs contact X-ray brachytherapy boost); these slides don't label which arm is which. The higher early good-response rate in one arm did **not** translate to a 5yr organ-preservation difference (see table).

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents rectal cancer treated with neoadjuvant therapy and considered for organ preservation. Does not represent partial responders (24%) or upfront-surgery candidates, in whom watch-and-wait was not offered.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: **Post-hoc**, non-randomised comparison of response groups; W14 selection **not prospectively validated** against the protocol W24 assessment. Single-trial, modest N; 5yr OP differences underpowered (**p=0.24**).

analysis_sections.label: Discussion
analysis_sections.body: Authors' RT-relevant read: **nCR reflects radiation change, not residual tumor**, and matches cCR for organ preservation (77% vs 81%), so an nCR should not trigger salvage TME. Early **DRE + rectoscopy** can move W&W selection up to W14 with MRI confirmation.

open_questions: Prospective validation of W14 vs W24 response assessment for W&W selection
Optimal RT boost technique to maximise early complete clinical response
Long-term regrowth risk in nCR pts managed by watch-and-wait

sites.intro: Two volume-and-fractionation reads: PEACE 2 fails to justify elective pelvic coverage in non-PSMA-staged very-high-risk N0, while HEAT's interim puts 36.25 Gy/5fx on non-inferior footing against 70.2 Gy/26fx.

studies.name: HEAT Trial
studies.tldr: AHRT 5fx non-inferior to EHRT 26fx for biochemical failure, 7% vs 7.4% (P_NI=0.007) at 4.25y; less acute GI toxicity.
details: 🔍 Phase III international non-inferiority RCT, 1:1; localized low- to intermediate-risk PCa, IPSS <12; ADT ≤6mo allowed both arms (NI margin 12%)
📊 AHRT non-inferior to EHRT for biochemical failure (Phoenix) at interim
📊 Acute G2+ GI toxicity lower with AHRT
📊 No significant difference in late G2+ GI or acute/late G2+ GU toxicity
⚠️ Interim analysis at ~1/3 of planned accrual; full pre-specified primary pending
🔗 First RCT comparing AHRT vs EHRT 1:1 with modern IMRT + ADT; prior ultrahypofx trials (HYPO-RT-PC, PACE-B, NRG-GU005) used heterogeneous or no-ADT controls
⚠️ Supportive-med use (laxatives, psyllium) counted as G2 — may inflate GI event rates

verdict.rationale: Interim analysis at 142 of 456 planned pts; non-inferiority met but full pre-specified primary analysis pending. Underpowered vs accrual goal.
verdict.audience: Localized low- to intermediate-risk prostate cancer, IPSS <12

studies.significance: The head-to-head prior SBRT trials skipped: 5fx AHRT (36.25 Gy, SIB 40 Gy) vs 26fx EHRT, both IMRT with ADT allowed, isolating fractionation from technique and hormones. Acute G2+ GI toxicity favored the 5fx arm at equal biochemical control, moving the ultrahypofractionation choice for low- to intermediate-risk pts.
studies.monday_clinic: In localized low- to intermediate-risk prostate with IPSS <12, this supports 5fx AHRT as non-inferior to 26fx EHRT with less acute GI toxicity; it does not extend to high-risk disease or glands >80cc, which the trial excluded.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Phase 3 international **non-inferiority RCT**, 1:1, biochemical failure (Phoenix) primary, NI margin 12%. **Interim analysis** at 142 analyzed of 456 planned; median follow-up 59.7 mo.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Localized **low- to intermediate-risk** prostate cancer, **IPSS <12**. 82.4% intermediate-risk; stratified by risk, gland volume (<60 vs 60-80cc), and ADT. 28% received ADT (≤6 mo, both arms).

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: **AHRT** 36.25 Gy/5 fx (7.25 Gy/fx) + GTV SIB to 40 Gy vs **EHRT** 70.2 Gy/26 fx (2.7 Gy/fx), IMRT in all patients.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: AHRT **non-inferior** to EHRT for biochemical failure: **7% vs 7.4%**, P non-inferiority=0.007 at 4.25y.

analysis_sections.label: Safety
analysis_sections.body: Acute G2+ GI toxicity **lower with AHRT**. No significant difference in late G2+ GI or acute/late G2+ GU toxicity. Supportive-med use (laxatives, psyllium) counted as G2.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: First RCT comparing AHRT vs EHRT 1:1 with modern IMRT and ADT. Prior ultrahypofractionation trials (HYPO-RT-PC, PACE-B, NRG-GU005) used heterogeneous or no-ADT controls.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents localized low- to intermediate-risk prostate cancer with IPSS <12. Does not represent high-risk disease or glands >80cc.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Interim analysis at roughly one-third of planned accrual; full pre-specified primary pending. Small analyzed N (142); biochemical, not clinical, endpoint.

open_questions: Durability of biochemical control at full accrual and longer follow-up
Does 5fx AHRT benefit extend to high-risk or larger-gland prostate?
Long-term late GU/GI toxicity with 5fx AHRT

studies.name: PEACE 2
studies.tldr: Pelvic nodal RT missed 1° endpoint: 7yr cPFS 67.1% vs 62.9% prostate-only, HR 0.81 (0.63-1.03) p=0.088; no MFS/PCSS/OS gain.
details: 📊 1° EP cPFS 7yr: 67.1% pelvic RT vs 62.9% prostate-only RT
📐 HR 0.81 (95% CI 0.63-1.03), p=0.088, non-significant (multivariable)
⚠️ Primary endpoint not met; cPFS trend favors pelvic but crosses 1
📊 No benefit on MFS, PCSS, or OS (authors' stated conclusion)
🔍 Phase III 2×2 factorial (ADT ×3yr all arms): pelvic vs prostate-only RT × cabazitaxel ×4

subdetails: N0M0 by conventional imaging or choline PET/CT, not PSMA

details: 🔗 POP-RT (2021): whole-pelvic RT improved biochemical control in PSMA-staged high-risk N0; PEACE 2 null
⚠️ N0 staged without PSMA, so occult nodal disease may be misclassified
⚠️ Conference data (ESTRO26), not yet peer-reviewed

verdict.rationale: Randomized phase 3, prespecified cPFS not met (p=0.088); null across all clinical endpoints diverges from POP-RT's whole-pelvic benefit, so the WPRT question is contested.
verdict.audience: cN0 very-high-risk localized prostate (Gleason≥8, T3-4, or PSA≥20)

studies.significance: The RT decision is elective nodal coverage: a randomized head-to-head (n=380 vs 381) shows whole-pelvic RT adds no cPFS, MFS, PCSS or OS benefit over prostate-only in cN0 very-high-risk disease, with modern-technique toxicity minimal. Staging was conventional imaging/choline PET, not PSMA, so PSMA-defined N0 pts remain untested.
studies.monday_clinic: In cN0 very-high-risk localized prostate staged by conventional imaging or choline PET, this questions routine elective whole-pelvic RT; it does not address PSMA-staged N0 or radiologic node-positive disease.

primary_endpoint.name: Clinical progression-free survival
primary_endpoint.stat_detail: 95% CI 0.63-1.03, p=0.088; primary endpoint not met

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Phase 3 randomized **2×2 factorial** (pelvic vs prostate-only RT × cabazitaxel ×4 vs none), ADT ×3 yr in all arms. Pelvic-RT comparison **n=380 vs 381**, 7-year outcomes reported. Multicenter GETUG/Unicancer.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: **Very high-risk localized** prostate: ≥2 of Gleason ≥8, T3-T4, PSA ≥20 ng/mL. **N0M0 by conventional imaging or choline PET/CT**, not PSMA.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: High-dose RT to **prostate vs whole pelvis**. Dose, fractionation and nodal target volume not reported in source.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary: clinical PFS.** Secondary: PSA response at 3 mo, bPFS, MFS, PCSS, OS, tolerance, QoL, biopsy biomarkers.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: cPFS 7yr **67.1% pelvic vs 62.9% prostate-only, HR 0.81 (0.63-1.03), p=0.088**, primary endpoint **not met**. No benefit on MFS, PCSS or OS per authors.

analysis_sections.label: Safety
analysis_sections.body: Authors report side effects **minimal with modern RT technique**; per-arm toxicity rates not reported in source.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: **POP-RT (2021)** found whole-pelvic RT improved biochemical control in PSMA-staged high-risk N0. PEACE 2's conventionally-staged cohort shows **no benefit**.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents cN0 very-high-risk localized prostate staged by conventional imaging or choline PET. Does not represent PSMA-staged N0 or radiologic node-positive disease.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Conference presentation, not peer-reviewed. **Non-PSMA staging** risks occult nodal misclassification. cPFS is a surrogate; RT dose/technique unspecified in source.

open_questions: Whether PSMA-staged N0 pts benefit from elective pelvic RT
Cabazitaxel's effect and interaction with pelvic RT
A nodal-risk subgroup that still benefits from pelvic RT

sites.intro: De-escalation by volume (INRT-AIR/DARTBOARD, 0% solitary elective nodal recurrence without a randomised contrast) and by modality (TORPEdO, no proton HR-QoL gain) both land short of practice-changing evidence.

studies.name: TORPEdO
studies.tldr: UW-QoL physical composite similar IMPT vs IMRT at 3/12/24mo in OPSCC; no proton QoL-toxicity benefit (no effect size reported in source).
details: 🔍 Phase 3 multicentre RCT (CRUK/18/010), N=205 OPSCC, 2:1 IMPT:IMRT; stratified by T-stage, N-stage, p16, smoking.
🔍 Both arms 70 Gy / 56 Gy in 33 fractions over 6.5 weeks; concurrent cisplatin 100 mg/m² D1+D22.
📊 PRO co-primary: UW-QoL physical composite (saliva, taste, chewing, swallowing, appearance, speech) at 12mo post-CRT.
📊 No between-arm difference in UW-QoL physical composite at 3/12/24mo, similar from 6 weeks post-CRT.
📊 Both arms dip at end of RT then recover; most stabilise by 12mo, some deterioration persists to 2yr.
⚠️ No effect sizes, CIs, or p-values reported in source; equivalence stated but magnitude not quantified.
⚠️ Clinician co-primary (grade 3 weight loss ≥20% or gastrostomy dependence at 12mo) not reported in this HR-QoL readout.
⚠️ Late-effects primary not mature; HR-QoL follow-up ongoing to 5yr post-CRT.
⚠️ Critique (@AmadeoWals): identical planning constraints + novel UK proton centres may cap IMPT's advantage; experienced centres report clinical gains.

verdict.rationale: Randomised co-primary QoL null (IMPT≈IMRT), consistent with IMRT-based CRT remaining standard. Only HR-QoL co-primary shown; clinician co-primary and 5yr late effects still maturing.
verdict.audience: Oropharyngeal SCC requiring bilateral-neck concurrent chemoradiotherapy

studies.significance: The proton premise, less QoL toxicity, didn't show: UW-QoL physical composite tracked identically IMPT vs IMRT from 6 weeks through 24 months, both arms at 70/56 Gy in 33fx. That undercuts routing OPSCC pts to protons for QoL benefit, though identical planning constraints and novel UK proton centres may have capped IMPT's achievable dosimetric edge.
studies.monday_clinic: In oropharyngeal SCC planned for definitive cisplatin chemoradiotherapy, these HR-QoL data give no patient-reported reason to prefer IMPT over IMRT; they don't address the clinician co-primary (weight loss/gastrostomy) or unilateral-neck cases that could still differ.

primary_endpoint.name: UW-QoL physical composite score at 12mo (PRO co-primary)
primary_endpoint.stat_detail: No effect size reported in source; assessed at 3/12/24mo post-RT

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: **Phase 3 multicentre RCT** (CRUK/18/010), N=205 oropharyngeal SCC, **2:1 IMPT:IMRT**, stratified by T-stage, N-stage, p16 status, and smoking history.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: **Oropharyngeal SCC requiring concurrent chemoradiotherapy with bilateral neck treatment**. p16 status was a stratification factor, so both HPV-associated and HPV-independent disease are included.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: **70 Gy / 56 Gy in 33 fractions over 6.5 weeks**, identical for both arms; IMPT vs IMRT is the only difference. Identical planning constraints applied across modalities.

analysis_sections.label: Regimen
analysis_sections.body: Concurrent **cisplatin 100 mg/m² on D1 and D22**.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Co-primary PRO:** UW-QoL physical composite at 12mo (saliva, taste, chewing, swallowing, appearance, speech). **Co-primary clinician:** CTCAE grade 3 weight loss (≥20% from baseline) or gastrostomy dependence at 12mo.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: **No between-arm difference** in UW-QoL physical composite at 3/12/24mo, similar from 6 weeks post-CRT, with similar trajectories across multiple PRO questionnaires. **No effect size reported in source.**

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: Support for protons in OPSCC has rested on dosimetric and single-institution toxicity data; this randomised HR-QoL readout does **not confirm a patient-reported advantage** for IMPT.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents oropharyngeal SCC treated with definitive concurrent cisplatin chemoradiotherapy and bilateral neck RT. Does not represent unilateral-neck, RT-alone, or non-oropharyngeal head-and-neck cancers.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Only the HR-QoL co-primary is presented; the clinician co-primary (weight loss/gastrostomy) and 5yr late-effects follow-up are unreported. No effect sizes or CIs given. Novel UK proton centres and identical constraints may cap IMPT's achievable edge.

open_questions: Does IMPT reduce 5-year late effects vs IMRT?
Clinician co-primary (weight loss/gastrostomy at 12mo) result?
Does IMPT advantage emerge only at high-experience proton centres?

studies.name: INRT-AIR & DARTBOARD pooled analysis
studies.tldr: 5-yr solitary elective nodal recurrence 0% with ENI omission across 117 pts; 5-yr OS 87%, PFS 74%.
details: 🔍 Patient-level pooled analysis of 2 prospective trials (INRT-AIR + DARTBOARD), N=117, median f/u 3.4 yrs
📐 Target volume: involved nodal RT (INRT) only, elective nodal levels omitted
🔍 AI model used to identify suspicious nodes for inclusion in the INRT volume
🔍 Eligibility: oropharynx, larynx, hypopharynx HNSCC; stage I-IVB, excluding T1-2N0 larynx
🔍 Required staging PET/CT plus neck CT before treatment
📊 Mean composite MDADI 84.9 at 12 months, no significant decline after treatment
⚠️ No RT dose or fractionation reported in source; transferability to a reader's INRT volume undefined
⚠️ No concurrent ENI comparator arm; the 0% solitary elective recurrence has no randomised contrast
⚠️ Median f/u 3.4 yrs vs 5-yr endpoints, so the 5-yr estimates rest on a thinning tail
⚠️ Toxicity reported only as MDADI swallowing; no G3+ late xerostomia, stricture, or feeding-tube data in source
🔗 Authors themselves gate implementation on randomised evidence, not these pooled results

verdict.rationale: Pooled single-arm prospective cohorts, N=117, no randomised ENI comparator; presenters explicitly require randomised evidence before non-trial use.
verdict.audience: Oropharynx/larynx/hypopharynx HNSCC, stage I-IVB, excl T1-2N0 larynx

studies.significance: The failure pattern is what matters: solitary elective nodal recurrence was 0% at 5 yrs while 3-yr local recurrence ran 9.5% and distant 11%, so the residual risk sits in the primary and systemically, not in the uncovered elective levels. Node selection was AI-assisted off PET/CT plus neck CT, which gates whether the volume reproduces outside these trials.
studies.monday_clinic: In PET/CT-staged oropharynx, larynx, or hypopharynx HNSCC (excluding T1-2N0 larynx) being planned for definitive chemoRT, this supports enrolling on an INRT trial rather than omitting elective nodal coverage off-protocol, and it does not speak to node-positive necks staged without PET/CT.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Patient-level pooled analysis of **two prospective trials** of involved nodal RT, INRT-AIR and DARTBOARD. **N=117**, median follow-up **3.4 years**. No randomised ENI comparator arm.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: HNSCC of **oropharynx, larynx, and hypopharynx**, stage **I-IVB**, excluding **T1-2N0 larynx**. Completed **PET/CT and neck CT** required for entry.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: **INRT omits elective nodal irradiation**, treating involved nodes only, with an **artificial-intelligence model** assisting identification of suspicious lymph nodes. Dose, fractionation, and margin not reported in source.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: **5-yr solitary elective nodal recurrence 0%.** 3-yr cumulative incidence: local **9.5%**, regional **4.3%**, distant **11%**. 5-yr OS **87%**, PFS **74%**.

analysis_sections.label: Safety
analysis_sections.body: Mean composite **MDADI 84.9 at 12 months**, with no significant decline after treatment. No late G3+ toxicity, xerostomia, or feeding-tube rates reported in source.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents PET/CT-staged oropharynx, larynx, and hypopharynx HNSCC, stage I-IVB, treated with definitive INRT on trial. Does not represent T1-2N0 larynx, oral cavity or nasopharynx primaries, or necks staged without PET/CT.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Pooled single-arm data with **no ENI control**, so the 0% solitary elective recurrence carries no randomised contrast. Median follow-up **3.4 yrs** underpins 5-yr estimates, and the AI nodal-selection step is not externally reproduced.

analysis_sections.label: Discussion
analysis_sections.body: The dosimetric case for INRT rests on OAR sparing, but the only patient-reported outcome here is swallowing (**MDADI 84.9**), with no xerostomia or dysphagia comparison against ENI. Authors state **randomized evidence is required** before non-trial implementation.

sites.intro: Pooled IPD puts EBRT in the same OS range as resection and ablation for BCLC-0/A, though the comparison is cross-study, not randomised.

studies.name: Multinational HCC EBRT IPD Cohort
studies.tldr: Median OS 6.8y BCLC-0 and 4.6y BCLC-A across 4,913 EBRT-treated HCC pts, comparable to resection/ablation.
details: 🔍 IPD pooled from published series meeting prespecified HCC technical standards; search date Dec 15 2022
🔍 N=4,913, median f/u 5.0 yrs, corresponding authors invited to contribute patient-level data

details.text: 📊 Median OS by BCLC stage and treatment status

details: 📊 Ablative dose associated with reduced mortality risk on multivariable Cox; HR not reported in source
📊 More recent treatment year also associated with reduced mortality risk

details.text: 📐 Higher mortality risk on random-effects multivariable Cox

details: ⚠️ No comparator arm: 'comparable with resection and ablation' is a cross-study read, not a randomised one
⚠️ IPD contributed by responding corresponding authors only; centers with worse outcomes may be underrepresented
⚠️ Technical-standard filter selects modern-technique series, so results may not transfer to non-specialist EBRT

verdict.rationale: Large multinational prospective-collected IPD across many sites with stage-stratified analyses, but non-randomised and no head-to-head comparator; supports EBRT's guideline-listed role rather than establishing it.
verdict.audience: Very early / early-stage HCC (BCLC-0 to A), Child-Pugh A predominant

studies.significance: The treatment-naive BCLC-0 arm is the RT-relevant cell: median OS not reached (95% CI 8.6 to NR), i.e. first-line EBRT in a truly untreated very-early cohort, not salvage after failed ablation. Ablative dose independently predicted lower mortality, so this moves the dose-selection and BCLC-algorithm-placement decisions.
studies.monday_clinic: In BCLC-0 or A HCC where ablation or resection is unattractive on lesion location or comorbidity, this supports offering ablative-dose EBRT as a first-line locoregional option rather than a salvage one; it does not speak to BCLC-B/C or Child-Pugh B-C pts, where OS was worse.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Systematic review (search date December 15, 2022) of EBRT series meeting **prespecified HCC technical standards** and reporting OS, followed by **individual patient data** solicitation from corresponding authors. Kaplan-Meier OS and RMST stratified by BCLC stage and treatment status; random-effects Cox for covariates.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: **N=4,913** EBRT-treated HCC pts across a multinational author network, **median follow-up 5.0 years**. Stratified as treatment-naive vs treatment-experienced; BCLC stage, tumor burden, performance status, and Child-Pugh class all captured at patient level.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: EBRT delivered per each contributing series; the pooling filter was **prespecified technical standards for HCC**. **Ablative dose was independently associated with reduced risk of death**, but specific dose/fractionation schedules and the ablative-dose threshold are not reported in the source abstract.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: Median OS **6.8 years for BCLC-0** and **4.6 years for BCLC-A**; among treatment-naive pts **not reached for BCLC-0** and **5.4 years for BCLC-A**. On multivariable modeling, advanced BCLC stage, higher tumor burden, worse performance status, and Child-Pugh B/C raised mortality risk; ablative dose and more recent treatment year lowered it.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: The authors position these OS figures as **comparable with resection, thermal ablation, and other ablative locoregional therapies**. That comparison is across literatures, not within a trial, so the usual selection differences (lesion location, vascular proximity, comorbidity burden) between an EBRT-referred and a resection-eligible population remain unadjusted.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents very early and early-stage HCC (BCLC-0 to A) treated with technically modern EBRT at centers publishing outcomes. Does not represent BCLC-B/C disease, Child-Pugh B-C liver function, or EBRT delivered outside the prespecified technical standards.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: **No comparator arm**: the resection/ablation equivalence claim is a cross-study inference. IPD came only from authors who responded, and the technical-standard filter selects for expert centers, both biasing toward better outcomes. Year-of-treatment effect confounds technique era with staging and systemic-therapy improvements.

analysis_sections.label: Discussion
analysis_sections.body: The practical argument here is **algorithmic placement**: BCLC has historically routed early HCC to resection, ablation, or transplant with EBRT absent, and this is the largest patient-level dataset assembled to contest that omission. The treatment-naive BCLC-0 result matters most, since it isolates first-line EBRT from salvage-after-ablation-failure use.

open_questions: Randomised EBRT vs thermal ablation in early HCC
Ablative dose threshold defining the OS benefit
Generalizability beyond expert EBRT centers

sites.intro: EXTEND is the largest randomised MDT dataset yet and the RT dose it used goes unreported, so transfer to your own SABR practice rests on the SABR-COMET-era schedules.

studies.name: EXTEND Trial
studies.tldr: MDT+SOC improved PFS across all baskets, HR 0.54 (0.41-0.72), p<0.001; RT delivered 98% of MDT.
details: 🔍 Multicenter randomized phase II basket trial, 1-5 mets, 6 baskets (breast, pancreas, kidney, 2 prostate, Other) with basket-specific stratification + powering
🔍 RT was the MDT for 98% of treated metastases (370/379); dose/fractionation/technique not reported in source abstract
📊 1° EP PFS, per-protocol set. Overall HR 0.54 (95% CI 0.41-0.72), p<0.001
📊 Excluding both prostate baskets: PFS HR 0.60 (95% CI 0.40-0.89), so the signal is not carried by prostate alone

details.text: 📊 Per-basket PFS read (source states direction only, no per-basket HRs given)
subdetails: Per-basket HRs/CIs not reported in source abstract

details: 📊 ctDNA: detectable at enrollment → shorter PFS and survival; clearance at 3mo → improved survival. No effect sizes reported in source
🔍 Screened 521 → randomized 350 → 334 analyzed per protocol (MDT+SOC n=166, SOC n=168). Median f/u 53mo
⚠️ 1° analysis pre-specified in the PER-PROTOCOL set, not ITT; 16/350 randomized pts (4.6%) excluded from analysis
⚠️ Phase II, open-label, PFS primary with no OS claim in source. Multiple baskets tested → multiplicity risk despite basket-specific powering
🔗 vs SABR-COMET (Lancet 2019, mixed histology, N=99, OS HR 0.57): EXTEND is larger and PFS-only but concordant in direction
🔗 vs STOMP/ORIOLE (prostate, small randomized phase II): EXTEND's prostate baskets extend the same MDT signal alongside SOC systemic therapy

verdict.rationale: Randomized phase II, primary PFS endpoint hit with 53mo f/u, but per-protocol analysis and basket design; histology signals explicitly framed as hypothesis-generating for phase III.
verdict.audience: Oligometastatic solid tumors, 1-5 mets, on standard systemic therapy

studies.significance: The RT-relevant read is that this is essentially an SBRT trial (98% of MDT was radiotherapy) layered on top of continued systemic therapy, so the PFS gain is attributable to local ablation, not a systemic switch. The prostate-excluded HR 0.60 (0.40-0.89) is what matters for referrals outside prostate. Dose and fractionation are not in the source abstract, which gates transfer to practice.
studies.monday_clinic: In pts with 1-5 metastases from pancreas or prostate primaries already on standard systemic therapy, this supports adding ablative MDT rather than systemic therapy alone; the breast and kidney baskets were inconclusive and do not carry the same read.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Multicenter **randomized phase II** basket trial, accrual 2018 to 2023. Six baskets (breast, pancreas, kidney, two prostate, "Other") each with **basket-specific stratification and powering**. Median follow-up **53 months**.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Patients with **1-5 metastases** from solid tumors, randomized to MDT+SOC vs SOC systemic therapy alone. Screened 521, randomized 350, **334 analyzed per protocol** (MDT+SOC n=166; SOC n=168).

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: **Radiotherapy was the MDT for 98% of metastases (370/379)**, so this reads as an ablative RT trial in all but name. Dose, fractionation, and technique are **not reported in the source abstract**.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary: PFS**, pre-specified in the **per-protocol set** within each basket, across all baskets, and across all baskets excluding prostate. Exploratory: **ctDNA** and immune profiling.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: Overall PFS **HR 0.54 (0.41-0.72), p<0.001**; excluding prostate baskets **HR 0.60 (0.40-0.89)**. Basket-level superiority in **pancreas, prostate, and "Other"**; breast and kidney **inconclusive**. Per-basket effect sizes not reported in source.

analysis_sections.label: Discussion
analysis_sections.body: Detectable ctDNA at enrollment tracked with shorter PFS and survival; **3-month ctDNA clearance** tracked with improved survival, raising the option of a ctDNA-refined oligometastatic definition. Systemic immune activation after MDT was most pronounced in the baskets that showed PFS superiority, offered as a **candidate mechanism**.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents patients with 1-5 metastases on standard systemic therapy, with pancreas, prostate, and "Other" histologies carrying the signal. Does not represent breast or kidney oligometastatic disease, where the baskets were inconclusive.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: **Per-protocol, not ITT**, primary analysis with 16 of 350 randomized patients excluded. **Phase II** with six baskets tested, so histology-specific claims are hypothesis-generating; no OS result reported in source.

open_questions: Does MDT-driven PFS gain translate to overall survival

sites.intro: FASTRACK II gives SABR its first prospective long-term local-control benchmark in inoperable primary RCC, still without a surgical or ablative comparator.

studies.name: FASTRACK II
studies.tldr: 100% freedom from local progression at 36, 60, and 84 months after single-fraction 26 Gy or 42 Gy/3fx SABR in inoperable primary RCC.
details: 📊 1° EP freedom from local progression: 100% at 36mo, 60mo, and 84mo (RECIST, ITT)
🔍 Non-randomised phase 2, 8 hospitals in Australia + Netherlands (TROG/ANZUP); 71 enrolled, 1 withdrew pre-treatment, 70 treated

subdetails: >4 cm: 42 Gy / 3 fx, delivered 48 h apart

details: 🔍 Eligibility: medically inoperable, high surgical risk, or declined surgery; ECOG ≤2; tumour ≤10 cm; N0-N1

details.text: 🔍 Baseline: median age 77 (70-82), median tumour size 46 mm (37-55), median f/u 62 mo (IQR 60-72)
⚠️ Treatment-related G3 AEs within 9 mo: 7 (10%) pts had ≥1 event

details: 📊 No grade 4 events, no treatment-related deaths, no new long-term safety signals
⚠️ Single-arm: no randomised comparator vs partial nephrectomy, cryoablation, or RFA in this operable-declining subset
⚠️ Colonic obstruction in 2/70 flags bowel as the dose-limiting OAR for larger central tumours on the 3fx schedule
⚠️ Median age 77 with competing mortality: 84-mo local control is the endpoint that matures fastest here; OS/cancer-specific survival not reported in source abstract beyond 'no cancer-related deaths'
🔗 Prior IROCK pooled retrospective series reported local control in the 97-98% range at 4-5 yr; this is the first prospective phase 2 confirmation with pre-planned long-term f/u

verdict.rationale: Single-arm phase 2; no randomised comparator vs nephrectomy or thermal ablation. Long f/u and 100% local control do not lift a non-randomised design.
verdict.audience: Primary RCC ≤10 cm, medically inoperable or declined surgery, N0-N1

studies.significance: The size-adapted prescription is what transfers: 26 Gy × 1 for ≤4 cm, 42 Gy/3fx at 48 h intervals above that, in a cohort that was 65% T1b or higher (median 46 mm), not a small-tumour-enriched series. Two colonic obstructions place bowel as the constraint for central lesions.
studies.monday_clinic: For a medically inoperable or surgery-declining pt with a T1b-T2a primary RCC ≤10 cm, this supports SABR as a definitive local option with durable 84-month local control; it does not address the surgically fit pt in whom partial nephrectomy remains untested against SABR.

primary_endpoint.name: Freedom from local progression
primary_endpoint.stat_detail: RECIST, intention-to-treat population; median f/u 62 mo

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: **Non-randomised phase 2**, eight hospitals in Australia and the Netherlands (TROG 15.03, with ANZUP). Accrual July 28 2016 to Feb 27 2020: **71 enrolled, one withdrew consent before treatment, 70 treated**. This report is the **pre-planned final long-term follow-up**, median **62 months (IQR 60-72)**.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Histologically confirmed primary RCC in pts who were **medically inoperable, high risk, or declined surgery**; ECOG **≤2**; tumour **≤10 cm**; **N0-N1**. Median age **77 (70-82)**, 70% male. Median tumour size **46 mm (37-55)**: T1a 34%, **T1b 56%**, T2a 9%, T3a 1%, with one N1 pt.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: Size-adapted prescription: **26 Gy in a single fraction** for tumours **≤4 cm**, and **42 Gy in 3 fractions delivered 48 h apart** for tumours **>4 cm**. No systemic therapy component.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary: freedom from local progression** by RECIST, assessed in the **intention-to-treat** population, with safety co-assessed in ITT.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: **100% local control at 36, 60, and 84 months**, with **no local recurrences and no cancer-related deaths** reported over median 62-month follow-up.

analysis_sections.label: Safety
analysis_sections.body: **Seven pts (10%)** had at least one treatment-related **grade 3** event within 9 months: pain (abdominal/flank/tumour) 4 (6%), nausea and vomiting 3 (4%), **colonic obstruction 2 (3%)**, diarrhoea 1 (1%). **No grade 4 events, no treatment-related deaths**, and no new long-term safety signals.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents non-surgical primary RCC ≤10 cm, predominantly T1b or higher, in a median-age-77 cohort. Does not represent surgically fit pts for whom partial nephrectomy is standard, or tumours >10 cm.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: **Single-arm and non-randomised**: no head-to-head against partial nephrectomy, cryoablation, or radiofrequency ablation, which is the live comparison in the surgery-declining subset. N=70 with **one N1 pt** leaves nodal disease essentially untested. Industry co-funding (Varian) alongside Cancer Australia.

analysis_sections.label: Discussion
analysis_sections.body: The value here is durability rather than magnitude: a **flat 100% at 84 months** in a cohort with **median 46 mm** tumours argues the ablative dose is adequate for lesions well beyond the small-renal-mass range where thermal ablation is usually deployed. The bowel events, not renal function, define the practical constraint for central tumours on the 3-fraction schedule.

open_questions: SABR vs partial nephrectomy in surgically fit pts
SABR vs thermal ablation for T1b tumours
Long-term renal function after single-fraction 26 Gy

sites.intro: NRG/RTOG 1005 delivers the practical win: WBI plus boost in 15 fractions, cosmesis and G3-4 toxicity unchanged.

studies.name: NRG/RTOG 1005
studies.tldr: 7-yr IBR 2.6% vs 2.2%, HR 1.31 (90% CI 0.84-2.04): concurrent boost noninferior to sequential, cutting WBI+boost to 15 fractions.
details: 📊 1° EP IBR as first recurrence: noninferiority met, upper 90% CI 2.04 below the 2.12 margin
🔍 Concurrent arm: WBI 40 Gy/15F with SIB 8 Gy/15F, 0.53 Gy per day to the cavity
🔍 Sequential arm: WBI 50 Gy/25F (52.4%) or 42.7 Gy/16F (47.6%), boost 12 Gy/6F in 84.9%

subdetails: 16.7% close (<2 mm) or focally positive margins

details: 📊 3-yr BCTOS cosmesis mean change 0.16 sequential vs 0.18 concurrent, noninferiority met (1-sided p<0.0001)
📊 No significant difference in grade 3-4 events between arms, p=0.81
⚠️ Superiority test for the concurrent arm, triggered by meeting NI, was not significant
⚠️ NI margin (HR 2.12) set under a protocol redesign assuming 1.59% 5-yr IBR; observed 2.1% and 1.9%

verdict.rationale: Randomized phase 3, prespecified noninferiority met at 7.3-yr median follow-up; wide NI margin (HR 2.12) and a point estimate favoring sequential keep it below practice-changing.
verdict.audience: High-risk early breast cancer post-lumpectomy, receiving WBI plus cavity boost

studies.significance: The transferable parameter is the boost itself: 8 Gy in 15F at 0.53 Gy per day, a lower cavity total than 12 Gy/6F sequential, with 7-yr IBR 2.6% vs 2.2%. Blinded photo cosmesis at 3y numerically favored concurrent (72.0% vs 64.2%, p=0.11). This moves boost technique, not whether to boost.
studies.monday_clinic: In high-risk early breast cancer after lumpectomy (grade 3, ER-negative, node-positive, or close margins) this supports a 15-fraction concurrent boost in place of a sequential boost; it does not address boost omission, partial breast irradiation, or post-mastectomy RT.

primary_endpoint.name: Ipsilateral breast recurrence as first recurrence

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Randomized, unblinded, **phase 3 noninferiority** trial across 278 sites in North America and 6 other countries. **2,354 randomized** May 2011 to June 2014, 2,255 eligible for analysis. Median follow-up **7.3 years**.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Higher risk of IBR after lumpectomy and axillary surgery. Median age 55 (IQR 47-64), 35.6% under 50; 96.8% invasive, **52.7% grade 3**, 29.6% ER-negative, **16.3% node-positive**, 16.7% LVI, 16.7% close (<2 mm) or focally positive margins. 61.8% received chemotherapy.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: Concurrent arm: WBI **40 Gy/15F** with **concurrent boost 8 Gy/15F** at 0.53 Gy per day. Sequential arm: WBI 50 Gy/25F (52.4%) or 42.7 Gy/16F (47.6%), then **12 Gy/6F** (84.9%) or 14 Gy/7F. 3DCRT in 59%, photons in 73.8%; QART scored 92.8% of contours and 91.9% of dose plans per protocol or acceptable variation.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary:** IBR as first recurrence, noninferiority defined as an HR upper 90% CI limit below **2.12**. Secondary: DFS, OS, adverse events, and cosmesis (BCTOS, physician Global Cosmesis Score, blinded central digital photo review).

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: 56 IBR events, **24 sequential and 32 concurrent**. Noninferiority held on cause-specific hazards, Fine-Gray (HR 1.31, 90% CI 0.84-2.03), and against each sequential WBI fractionation separately; the triggered superiority test was **not significant**. 7-yr regional nodal recurrence 1.2% (95% CI 0.8-1.7) with no difference by arm, and no differences in DFS, DDFS, or OS.

analysis_sections.label: Safety
analysis_sections.body: Toxicity above grade 2 was uncommon, with **no significant difference in grade 3-4 events (p=0.81)**; radiation dermatitis, fatigue, and breast pain were most prevalent. 3-yr excellent/good cosmesis by physician rating 85.9% vs 82.4% (p=0.34) and by central photo review 64.2% vs 72.0% (p=0.11).

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: IMPORT HIGH tested a simultaneous integrated boost in 15 fractions against a sequential boost in the same setting; its effect sizes are not in this source. This is the larger North American test of the same question, enriched for grade 3 and node-positive disease.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents high-risk early breast cancer after breast-conserving surgery receiving whole-breast irradiation plus a cavity boost. Does not represent pts considered for boost omission, partial breast irradiation, or post-mastectomy radiation.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Unblinded. The noninferiority margin (**HR 2.12**) was set under a protocol redesign against an assumed 1.59% 5-yr IBR, and the point estimate **favored sequential** (HR 1.31) on 56 total events. Cosmesis response rates fell to 50.5% (physician) and 47.1% (central photo) by 3 years.

open_questions: Durability of the concurrent boost beyond 7 years
Whether a concurrent boost transfers to regional nodal irradiation
Boost omission versus concurrent boost in high-risk pts

Five-year Results of the OPERA Trial: When and How to
Assess Tumor Response to Guide Rectal Preservation
Background & Methods
o When organ preservation (OP) is the goal evaluating tumor response after
neoadjuvant therapy (NAT) is a critical step in the decision. Achieving OP first
requires obtaining a complete clinical response (cCR)
o In the OPERA trial: tumour response was assessed at week 24 (W24) using a
three-modality approach : digital rectal examination (DRE), rectoscopy, and MRI.
This triad determined CR and eligibility for OP
• This post-hoc analysis evaluates earlier decision (W14) and clinical tumor response
evaluation (CTRE) through DRE and rectoscopy.
o At week 14: CTRE defined CR, nCR (near complete response) and PR (partial
response) "good" clinical response (CR) included cCR+nCR. MRI used TRG. Good
responders were considered for OP or reassessed at week 24. CTRE correlated with
relapse and 5-year OP rates.

Background & Methods
Background
• AHRT/SBRT (5 fx) is increasingly used in localized PCa
• Randomized phase Ill data on AHRT remains limited
• Limited IMRT use (HYPO-RT-PC*2)
• Heterogeneous control arms (PACE-B,34 NRG-GU005°)
• No ADT allowed (HYPO-RT-PC,12 PACE-B,3-4 NRG-GU005°)
• No known trial compares AHRT & EHRT 1:1 with modern techniques + ADT
Methods
• HEAT: International phase Ill randomized non-inferiority study
• AHRT: 36.25 Gy in 5 fractions, GTV SIB to 40 Gy
• EHRT: 70.2 Gy in 26 fractions using IMRT in all patients
• Localized low-intermediate risk PCa; ADT allowed in both arms (≤6 months)
• Primary endpoint: Biochemical failure (Phoenix definition)
• Non-inferiority margin = 12%
• Interim analysis presented

PEACE 2: Specific conclusion on pelvic RT
• Pelvic radiotherapy leads to a modest non-significant trend in improving cPFS
in very high-risk prostate cancer patients treated with high dose radiotherapy
and long-term hormonal therapy

of care in the treatment of prostate cancer patients, even at high risk of nodal
extension but no detectable disease on imaging.

Background & Methods
• Definitive chemoradiotherapy for HNSCC carries significant long-term
toxicities, with elective nodal irradiation (ENI) as a major contributor of
dose to OARs
• Involved nodal radiotherapy (INRT) is a novel approach that omits ENI,
with potential to reduce RT-induced toxicities and immunosuppression
• We conducted a patient-level pooled analysis from 2 prospective trials
studying INRT: INRT-AIR and DARTBOARD
• The novel INRT approach assisted by an artificial-intelligence model to
identify suspicious lymph nodes
• Eligibility: HNSCC of oropharynx, larynx, and hypopharynx; stage I-IVB
(excluding T1-2N0 larynx); completed PET/CT and neck CT

papers.title: Concurrent Versus Sequential Radiation Dose Escalation to the Surgical Cavity for Conservative Treatment of High-Risk Early Breast Cancer: NRG/RTOG 1005 Phase III Trial.
papers.journal: Journal of clinical oncology : official journal of the American Society of Clinical Oncology
papers.abstract:
PURPOSE: For patients with breast cancer undergoing breast conservation, escalating the dose (boost) of radiation to the lumpectomy cavity after whole-breast irradiation (WBI) reduces ipsilateral breast recurrence (IBR) but extends treatment duration. This phase III trial investigated whether boost delivery during WBI versus after WBI provides noninferior IBR and preserves cosmetic appearance.

METHODS: NRG/RTOG 1005 randomly assigned patients at higher risk for IBR after lumpectomy and axillary surgery to either a sequential boost of 12 Gy in six fractions(F) or 14 Gy in 7F after WBI of 50 Gy in 25F or 42.7 Gy in 16F (sequential arm) or a concurrent boost of 8 Gy in 15F of 0.53 Gy per day with WBI of 40 Gy in 15F (concurrent arm) using 3-dimensional conformal radiation therapy (RT) or intensity-modulated RT. Based on 1.59% 5-year IBR for the sequential arm, defining the noninferiority margin as a hazard ratio upper limit on the 90% CI of 2.12, 2,312 patients provide 80% power for noninferiority of IBR as first recurrence for the concurrent arm. Secondary end points included disease-free survival and overall survival, adverse events (AEs), and cosmetic outcomes.

RESULTS: Between May 24, 2011, and June 20, 2014, 2,354 patients were randomly assigned, with 2,255 eligible for analysis (sequential arm, n = 1,118; concurrent arm, n = 1,137). With median follow-up of 7.3 years, there were 56 IBR events; 5- and 7-year IBR were 2.1% and 2.2% on the sequential arm and 1.9% and 2.6% on the concurrent arm, respectively. The noninferiority criterion was met: HR (90% CI): 1.31 (0.84 to 2.04), P = .037. No differences were observed in AEs, cosmetic outcomes, or survival between arms.

CONCLUSION: Concurrent boost during WBI results in noninferior IBR compared with sequential boost without worsening toxicity or cosmetic outcomes and reduces overall treatment time.
papers.note: Vicini FA, Winter K, Freedman GM, Arthur DW, Rosenstein BS, Bentzen SM, Li XA, Halyard MY, Woodward WA, Bleicher RJ, Taghian A, Lyons J, Tomberlin JK, Seaward SA, Cheston SB, Hoover AC, Anderson BM, Perera FE, Poppe MM, Petersen IA, Jhawar S, Hijal T, Moughan J, Movsas B, White JR. Concurrent Versus Sequential Radiation Dose Escalation to the Surgical Cavity for Conservative Treatment of High-Risk Early Breast Cancer: NRG/RTOG 1005 Phase III Trial. J Clin Oncol. 2026 May 11:JCO2502465. ; PMCID: PMC13166090.

papers.title: Overall Survival Among Patients With Hepatocellular Carcinoma Treated With External Beam Radiation Therapy: Individual Patient Data Outcomes From a Multinational Cohort.
papers.journal: Journal of clinical oncology : official journal of the American Society of Clinical Oncology
papers.abstract:
PURPOSE: External beam radiation therapy (EBRT) has gained delayed acceptance as a recommended first-line treatment modality for patients with hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), given limited evidence that it improves overall survival (OS). We analyzed individual patient data (IPD) from an international cohort to assess OS among patients with HCC treated with EBRT.

METHODS: We performed a systematic review of publications that assessed EBRT, met prespecified technical standards for HCC, and reported OS (search date December 15, 2022). Corresponding authors were invited to submit IPD for the study. We performed Kaplan-Meier survival analyses to determine OS and restricted mean survival time (RMST) stratified by Barcelona Clinic Liver Cancer (BCLC) stage and treatment status (ie, treatment-naïve and experienced). We performed random effects Cox proportional hazards modeling to assess clinical characteristics associated with OS.

RESULTS: Data were provided on 4,913 patients treated with EBRT with a median follow-up time of 5.0 years. The median OS was 6.8 years (95% CI, 5.7 to 8.7) for BCLC-0 and 4.6 years (95% CI, 4.1 to 5.1) for BCLC-A. Among treatment-naïve patients, the median OS was not reached (95% CI, 8.6 to not reached) for BCLC-0 and was 5.4 years (95% CI, 4.5 to 6.7) for BCLC-A. In multivariable models, more advanced BCLC stage, higher tumor burden, worse performance status, and Child-Pugh class B or C were associated with a higher risk of mortality. Ablative radiation dose and more recent year of treatment were associated with a reduced risk of death.

CONCLUSION: To our knowledge, this study represents the largest multinational cohort of patients with HCC treated with EBRT. OS outcomes with EBRT for very early- and early-stage HCC appear to be comparable with resection, thermal ablation, and other ablative locoregional therapies. These data support the inclusion of EBRT in the BCLC HCC clinical decision-making process.
papers.note: Moon AM, Yanagihara TK, Dawson LA, Yu JI, Lawrence TS, Kim TH, Yan M, Iwata H, Nabavizadeh N, Apisarnthanarax S, Dunne EM, Lock MI, Chuong MD, Chiang CL, Scorsetti M, Katoh N, Sioshansi S, Numata K, Liu HY, Iwamoto H, Wakatsuki M, Chen Y, Pollom EL, Gkika E, Jabbour SK, Munoz-Schuffenegger P, Dutta D, Hajj C, Ueno M, Hallemeier CL, Feldman AM, Méndèz Romero A, Tan X, Molla M, Tepper JE, Torres F, Reig M; EBRT Collaboration Group. Overall Survival Among Patients With Hepatocellular Carcinoma Treated With External Beam Radiation Therapy: Individual Patient Data Outcomes From a Multinational Cohort. J Clin Oncol. 2026 May 15:JCO2502399.

papers.title: Addition of Metastasis-Directed Therapy to Standard of Care for Oligometastatic Solid Tumors: Primary Analysis of All Tumor-Histology Baskets of the Phase II Randomized EXTEND Trial.
papers.journal: Journal of clinical oncology : official journal of the American Society of Clinical Oncology
papers.abstract:
PURPOSE: We tested the hypothesis that adding metastasis-directed therapy (MDT) to standard of care (SOC) systemic therapy improves progression-free survival (PFS) among patients with oligometastatic disease.

METHODS: EXTEND was a multicenter randomized phase II trial. Patients with 1-5 metastases were randomized to MDT+SOC vs SOC in 1 of 6 baskets (breast, pancreas, kidney, two prostate baskets, and an "Other" basket) with basket-specific stratification and powering. PFS, the primary endpoint, was pre-specified in the per-protocol set within each basket, across all baskets, and across all baskets excluding the prostate baskets. Exploratory endpoints included circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) and immune profiling.

RESULTS: From 2018 through 2023, 521 patients were screened, 350 were randomized, and 334 were analyzed per protocol (MDT+SOC, n=166; SOC, n=168). Radiotherapy was used as MDT for 98% of metastases (370/379). Overall, after median follow-up of 53 months, PFS was improved with MDT+SOC (HR 0.54, 95% CI 0.41 to 0.72, p < 0.001). Similarly, PFS was improved when excluding the prostate baskets (HR, 0.60; 95% CI: 0.40 to 0.89). Within each basket, PFS superiority was identified for the pancreas, prostate, and "Other" baskets, whereas the breast and kidney baskets were inconclusive. At enrollment, detectable ctDNA correlated with shorter PFS and survival; by contrast, ctDNA clearance 3-months post-enrollment correlated with improved survival. MDT+SOC-induced systemic immune activation was most pronounced among baskets demonstrating PFS superiority.

CONCLUSION: The phase II EXTEND trial supports the addition of MDT to SOC for oligometastatic disease. Histology-specific efficacy signals were identified for phase III testing. Translational insights suggest the potential for optimizing the definition of oligometastasis using ctDNA, and point to systemic immune responses as a possible mechanism of benefit from MDT.

Addition of Metastasis-Directed Therapy to Standard of Care for Oligometastatic Solid Tumors: Primary Analysis of All Tumor-Histology Baskets of the Phase II Randomized EXTEND Trial. J Clin Oncol. 2026 May 16:101200JCO2502856.

papers.title: Long-term outcomes of stereotactic ablative body radiotherapy for primary kidney cancer (TROG 15.03 FASTRACK II): a multicentre, non-randomised, phase 2 study.
papers.abstract:
BACKGROUND: Stereotactic ablative body radiotherapy (SABR) is an emerging, non-invasive alternative for primary renal cell carcinoma. We aimed to provide the final long-term trial outcomes of TransTasman Radiation Oncology Group (TROG) 15.03 FASTRACK II, the first phase 2 trial investigating SABR for primary renal cell carcinoma to our knowledge.

METHODS: FASTRACK II was a non-randomised, phase 2 study conducted in eight hospitals in Australia and the Netherlands by TROG and the Australian and New Zealand Urogenital and Prostate (ANZUP) Cancer Trials Group. Here, we report the final pre-planned follow-up results. Adult patients (aged ≥18 years) with histologically confirmed primary renal cell carcinoma, who were medically inoperable, high risk, or declined surgery, had an Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group performance status of 2 or less, had tumours 10 cm or less in size, and had N0-N1 disease were included. Patients underwent either a single fraction SABR of 26 Gy for tumours 4 cm or less in maximum diameter, or 42 Gy in three fractions delivered 48 h apart for tumours more than 4 cm in maximum diameter. The primary outcome was freedom from local progression to assess local control after SABR evaluated with the Response Evaluation Criteria in Solid Tumours. The primary endpoint and safety were evaluated in the intention-to-treat population. A patient representative was involved in the study design and conduct. The trial was registered with ClinicalTrials.gov (NCT02613819) and is closed to enrolment.

FINDINGS: Between July 28, 2016, and Feb 27, 2020, 71 patients were enrolled and one withdrew consent before treatment. Median follow-up was 62 months (IQR 60-72), median age was 77 years (70-82). 49 (70%) of 70 patients were male and 21 (30%) were female. Race and ethnicity data were not collected. The median tumour size was 46 mm (37-55), with 24 (34%) patients with T1a disease, 39 (56%) with T1b disease, six (9%) with T2a disease, and one (1%) with T3a disease. One patient (1%) had nodal involvement (N1). SABR resulted in 100% local control at 36 months, 60 months, and 84 months. Seven (10%) patients had at least one grade 3 adverse event within 9 months of SABR that was designated possibly, probably, or definitely related to treatment: nausea and vomiting (three [4%] events); abdominal, flank, or tumour pain (four [6%]); colonic obstruction (two [3%]); and diarrhoea (one [1%]). No new long-term safety signals, grade 4 events, or treatment-related deaths were noted.

INTERPRETATION: Long-term follow-up supports the safety and local control of SABR for non-surgical patients with renal cell carcinoma, with no observed local recurrences or cancer-related deaths in this cohort, which had predominantly T1b disease or higher.
